name: Core Technical Principles
description: Fundamental technical knowledge including forces, materials categories, and the impact of new and emerging technologies
commonMisconceptions: All plastics are harmful to the environment (some bioplastics are biodegradable and derived from renewable sources)
Smart materials and composite materials are the same thing (smart materials respond to stimuli; composites combine two or more materials for improved properties)
CAD and CAM are the same process (CAD is computer-aided design; CAM is computer-aided manufacture)

name: Specialist Technical Principles
description: In-depth knowledge of material-specific properties, selection criteria and standard components
commonMisconceptions: One-off production is always more expensive per unit than mass production (while unit cost is higher, tooling costs are much lower)
Hardwood always comes from deciduous trees and softwood from coniferous trees (the classification is botanical, not about physical hardness)
All metals rust (only ferrous metals containing iron rust; non-ferrous metals corrode differently)

name: Materials & Their Properties
description: Physical and working properties of woods, metals, polymers, textiles, papers and boards, and composites
commonMisconceptions: Hardness and toughness are the same property (a hard material resists scratching; a tough material resists fracture under impact)
Thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics can both be reheated and reshaped (only thermoplastics can; thermosets undergo permanent chemical change)
All composites are modern inventions (concrete and plywood are traditional composite materials)

name: Manufacturing Processes
description: Techniques for shaping, joining, cutting and forming materials including both traditional and modern methods
commonMisconceptions: 3D printing is suitable for all scales of production (it is mainly used for prototyping and one-off production; it is too slow for mass production)
Welding and soldering are the same process (welding fuses base materials together; soldering uses a filler metal with a lower melting point)
All wood joints require adhesive to be strong (mechanical joints like dovetails can hold firmly without glue)

name: Energy & Systems
description: Mechanical, electrical and electronic systems, including inputs, processes and outputs
commonMisconceptions: A closed-loop system and an open-loop system are equally accurate (closed-loop systems use feedback to self-correct, making them more accurate)
Gears only change speed (they can also change direction of rotation and increase or decrease torque)
Electronic components and electrical components are the same (electronic components use semiconductors and signal processing; electrical components use mains current)

name: Design Strategies
description: Approaches to solving design problems including user-centred design, systems thinking and collaboration
commonMisconceptions: The design brief and the specification are the same thing (the brief outlines the problem; the specification details measurable criteria the solution must meet)
Primary research is always better than secondary research (both have value; secondary research can provide broader context more efficiently)
Design fixation only affects inexperienced designers (even experienced designers can become fixated on initial ideas)

name: Iterative Design
description: The cyclical process of designing, prototyping, testing and refining products
commonMisconceptions: The design process is always linear (it is iterative, meaning designers regularly revisit and refine earlier stages)
Prototyping is only done once near the end of a project (prototypes should be made and tested throughout the design process)
CAD replaces the need for hand sketching (initial ideas are often fastest to explore with quick hand sketches)

name: Evaluation & Testing
description: Methods of testing materials and products against specifications, including quality control
commonMisconceptions: Quality control and quality assurance are the same thing (QC checks individual products; QA is the overall system that ensures consistent quality)
Evaluation only happens at the end of a project (ongoing evaluation throughout the design process leads to better outcomes)
If a product works, it has met the specification (it must also meet aesthetic, cost, safety and environmental criteria)

name: New Technologies
description: The impact of modern technologies, sustainability and environmental considerations on product design

subskills.name: Impact of Technology on People and Society

commonMisconceptions: Recycling solves all environmental problems (reducing consumption and reusing products are more effective strategies)
Automation always leads to job losses (it can also create new roles in programming, maintenance and design)
Planned obsolescence is always unethical (sometimes updated products offer genuine safety or efficiency improvements)

name: Design Communication
description: Methods of communicating design ideas through drawing, modelling, annotation and digital tools
commonMisconceptions: Isometric and perspective drawing are the same thing (isometric uses parallel lines at 30 degrees; perspective uses vanishing points)
CAD drawings do not need annotation (all design drawings should include notes on materials, dimensions and manufacturing)
First angle and third angle projection show different views of an object (they show the same views arranged differently on the page)
